item.title: I really *want* to like this...
item.content:encoded:
…and I'll upvote it, but the heavy-handed depiction of Westhead Media was a little too unsubtle for my taste, and there are asides that feel like plot holes. For example, the nutrient mix that stimulates creativity is either anomalous or non-anomalous; since we as readers are aware that it doesn't exist in the real world, we should receive a little more information about it, to clarify whether the supplement is an everyday vitamin in the world of SCP-3149 or an unusual product that everyday people would find as astonishing as an anomaly.

So, +1, but with reservations. I feel like this could easily be improved by an order of magnitude with a manageable amount of revision.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The second image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to the presence of an asset which, in its license, contains "NC". Thus, the second image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just, fantastic. The combination of monkey and book based puns along with actually really kinda unsettling writing just gives me joy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the upvote, and I've taken your advice and added some crosslinks to the Three Portlands hub and (from what I can tell) the first mention of Backdoor So-Ho!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, now that is a disturbing take on the Infinite monkey theorem. I really enjoyed reading this. It's bizarre and delightfully creepy. +1 for that. That said, I would recommend you put hyper links on "including Three Portlands, the state of Lee, and Backdoor So-Ho." so that readers know what you're referring to. I vaguely know of Three Portlands, but I know it has a hub you can link to, I know literally nothing of Backdoor So-Ho, but its at least a tag on the site, and finally the state of lee might be your own invention as far as I know. Part of the point of referencing GOI's and other such concepts is to get new readers to check them out. Throw those writers a bone and give them a link if you can. That's just my suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the monkey, thought, "Oh boy, this ought to be good."

Then, I read, "YOU DO THE HOKEY POKEY AND YOU TURN AROUND."

Exceptional work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Must've missed it.

Then again, I'm thinking of bare-bones narrative with random gibberish mixed in. Kinda like…

So he said "fn cm nvdf" and the bvghnb caused gf D dcvgh h to collapse under them.

You can't say that's not a narrative. It's got an action and people talking. It's not remotely sellable and no editor can rewrite it to make sense, but it doesn't seem like the bookworms care about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Putting his wand back into his pocket, Harry sighed and stuffed his wand back into his pocket

Ron's Ron shirt was almost as bad as Ron himself.

I'm glad the Bookworms were named in the narrative, because they were the one element I didn't get. Well, and I guess I don't get why they're still working if the woman is dead. I guess because the Bookworms continue to be enforcers? +1 regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…if a monkey/author decided to "game the system" and started to produce literal zero-effort narratives…

I'm pretty sure that's exactly what happened with the Harry Potter excerpt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… So, what happened if a monkey/author decided to "game the system" and started to produce literal zero-effort narratives (or just worked on a massive, never-ending zero effort narrative so it never went into the chute)?

Yes, a page every 10 minutes for 19 hours per day is grueling, but it doesn't look like there's mention of any controls to make the monkey's output actually something worth selling/profitable. Or stopping them from writing a never-ending narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand what you're saying here, but I just want to explain my reasoning here a little. The use of monkeys was, to me while writing, intended to be another layer of dehumanization towards the authors on the part of Westhead. Not only are they being forced to work in abysmal conditions, in bodies that aren't even theirs, but they're not even human bodies. Its humiliation, another way to break them down and make them do what they're told.

It's fine if that aspect of the skip didn't quite work for you, but I just wanted to explain where I was coming from with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The literal usage of Monkeys banging away at typewriters pulls this away from what could have been a clever satire of the publishing industry and a reflection on the relationship between authors and their most popular works, instead pushing it firmly into talking animal territory, but the interview log was entertaining enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like somebody already got that one, but thank you for pointing it out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3149-2 specimens are docile towards individuals from outside SCP-XXX unless one attempts to remove them, at which point they will become hostile until allowed to return to their original position.

u missed an XXX, i would fill it in but i dont know what its referring to

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to TheGreatSea, Westrin, PeppersGhost, NineVolt, DarkStuff and CaptainKirby.

Another thanks to PeppersGhost for contributing the second image!

IMAGE CREDITS - PLEASE DON’T SUE TANHONY

Second Image: Made using https://www.flickr.com/photos/roland/704338/ by Roland Tanglao and https://www.flickr.com/photos/24256658@N06/16868272087/ by ‘tomosuke214’.
